verified_bugs.description: `_read_xref_subsections` iterates `range(start, start + size)` where `size` comes from the PDF's attacker-controlled `/Index` or `/Size` entry in the xref stream. There is no upper bound on `size`. When the underlying BytesIO data is exhausted, `get_entry` returns 0 rather than terminating the loop, so the iteration continues to completion. A malicious PDF declaring e.g. `/Index [0 4000000000]` triggers multi-billion-iteration CPU consumption.
verified_bugs.reachability: Directly reachable via `PdfReader(untrusted_pdf)` during initial xref parsing — no authentication, no special flags required. This is the primary entry point of the library.
verified_bugs.exploitability: A single crafted PDF delivered to any application that calls `PdfReader` on user-supplied input causes hang/OOM. No attacker-side computation needed beyond crafting a tiny xref stream with a large `/Index` or `/Size` value.
verified_bugs.suggested_fix: Validate `size` against a sane cap (PDF 32000 spec implies ~8.4M objects max) before entering the inner loop, and/or cap the cumulative number of xref entries processed. Raise PdfReadError when exceeded.
verified_bugs.status: ALREADY PATCHED — fixed 2026-04-14 in commit 62338e9d (PR #3733: 'SEC: Limit the allowed size of xref and object streams'). New function `_sanitize_pdf15_xref_stream_index_pairs` clamps /Index counts to fit actual stream data length. Not eligible for a new CVE.

false_positives.reason: File.__str__/__repr__ are Python debug dunders; pypdf has no HTML/JSON rendering surface. json.dumps does not call __str__. No reachable sink.
_write_field emits a plain-text debug report to a caller-supplied fileobj. pypdf has no HTML context; XSS claim has no sink.
Returns list[PageObject]; never emits strings. No HTML context. Broad except merely normalizes type errors.
Function only builds an in-memory dict. No filesystem operations occur; path traversal is not reachable within this function.
CLI argument parser. Input is developer-supplied argv, not PDF-derived. No HTML output sink.

Empty passwords are explicitly valid per PDF spec (default user password). Hash is still compared against stored u_value/o_value; empty password only succeeds for PDFs encrypted with one. Cited CVE is an unrelated web-auth issue.

AES-256-ECB for the Perms block is mandated by ISO 32000 Algorithm 3.10. Block is exactly 16 bytes (one AES block), so ECB pattern-leakage does not apply. Changing the mode would break spec conformance.
pypdf decrypts local files on the user's own machine. No network oracle exists; offline attackers can brute force without caring about timing. No remote attack surface.

Python 3's open() raises ValueError on embedded null bytes natively. Null-byte path traversal is a C/PHP-era bug not reachable in Python 3. Additionally, paths are supplied by the library consumer, not PDF content.
